---
title: "The Social Cost of Carbon"
subtitle: "A playful poll through a serious number · TTT"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    include-after-body: poll-deck.html
---

## Join in {data-room-join="true"}

Scan to vote from your phone — answers follow the slides.

::: {.join-box}
<!-- QR + live "phones connected" count render here -->
:::

::: {.notes}
Wait here until the join count climbs. People can stay on the page the whole time —
questions appear automatically as we go.
:::

## Why put a price on carbon?

Climate policy is a **cost–benefit problem**:

- Cutting emissions costs something **today**.
- It avoids damages **tomorrow** — heat, floods, lost crops, lives.

To weigh them we need one number: the **price of a tonne of CO₂**.

::: {.notes}
This number quietly underlies trillions in regulatory decisions. Today we poke at how
shaky — and how high — it might really be.
:::

## The Social Cost of Carbon

> The present value of the damages from emitting one extra tonne of CO₂.

It's the workhorse behind federal cost–benefit analysis. A higher SCC justifies more
aggressive climate policy.

## Your gut: what's a tonne of CO₂ worth? {data-poll="q1"}

::::: {.columns}
:::: {.column width="50%"}
- **A.** Under \$25
- **B.** Around \$50
- **C.** Around \$185
- **D.** Over \$300
::::
:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.poll-chart}
:::
::::
:::::

::: {.notes}
No reveal yet — let them commit to a guess. Most will lowball it.
:::

## Now pin it down {data-poll="q4"}

::::: {.columns}
:::: {.column width="50%"}
Type the **exact** social cost of CO₂ you'd defend, in **US\$ per tonne**, on your phone.

No bins this time — give me a real number.
::::
:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.poll-chart}
:::
::::
:::::

::: {.notes}
Watch the spread. Big variance is the point — there's no consensus number. Then reveal.
:::

## Rennert et al. (2022): a higher number

In *Nature* — "Comprehensive evidence implies a higher social cost of CO₂":

- Central estimate: **\$185 per tonne** (2% discount rate).
- **3.6×** the \$51 the U.S. government was using.
- Range roughly **\$44 – \$413** (5th–95th percentile).

Built by updating four things at once: socioeconomics, climate, damages, discounting.

::: {.notes}
Did the room under- or over-shoot? Why might the true number be even messier than a
single point estimate? → segue to Weitzman.
:::

## Weitzman's Dismal Theorem {data-poll="q2"}

::::: {.columns}
:::: {.column width="50%"}
With fat-tailed catastrophic risk, the "correct" carbon price could be…

- **A.** A modest, finite number
- **B.** Effectively unbounded
- **C.** Zero — too uncertain to act
- **D.** Whatever the discount rate implies
::::
:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.poll-chart}
:::
::::
:::::

::: {.notes}
This is the provocation. Let the split show before explaining.
:::

## Fat tails break the tidy number

Weitzman (2009): if catastrophe sits in a **fat tail** and marginal utility blows up as
consumption collapses, expected damages can be **unbounded** — standard cost–benefit
"breaks down."

- A single \$185 hides enormous tail risk.
- The argument: **structural uncertainty** can matter more than the discount rate.

::: {.notes}
Invite disagreement — economists push back hard. Is an unbounded price useful for actual
policy? Let it breathe.
:::

## What will we *actually* hit? {data-poll="q3"}

::::: {.columns}
:::: {.column width="50%"}
Peak warming vs. pre-industrial — your honest bet:

- **A.** 1.5 °C or less
- **B.** Around 2 °C
- **C.** Around 3 °C
- **D.** 4 °C or more
::::
:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.poll-chart}
:::
::::
:::::

## Put a number on it {data-poll="q5"}

::::: {.columns}
:::: {.column width="50%"}
On your phone: your single best guess for **peak warming in °C** above pre-industrial.
::::
:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.poll-chart}
:::
::::
:::::

::: {.notes}
Contrast the room's median with 1.5/2 °C targets and the SCC numbers we saw. Pivot to
discussion.
:::

## From a number to a conversation

- The SCC is the **bridge** from climate science to policy.
- Rennert et al.: a defensible **\$185** — up from \$51.
- Weitzman: maybe no single number can hold the **tail risk**.
- What we *expect* to hit shapes what we're willing to pay now.

*Source: Rennert et al., Nature 610, 687–692 (2022); Weitzman, REStat 91 (2009).*

::: {.notes}
Open floor: does a higher SCC change anyone's mind? Where should the number live — point
estimate, range, or something that takes tail risk seriously?
:::
